project_overview.project_development_objective: To strengthen institutional capacity for financial stability and to enhance access to finance for micro, small and medium enterprises
project_overview.beneficiaries: Da Afghanistan Bank (DAB), Afghan Deposit Insurance Corporation (ADIC), Afghan Credit Guarantee Foundation (ACGF), Participating Financial Institutions (PFIs), MSMEs including women-owned businesses

geographic_scope.coverage_type: national with focus on five major cities
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - Afghanistan is a fragile and conflict-affected state with ongoing violent conflict, security challenges, and political instability

key_components.description: Capital contribution to ACGF (US$15M) to expand partial credit guarantees to PFIs for MSME lending, plus technical assistance (US$5M) to strengthen ACGF operations and PFI capacity
Matching grants to SMEs for fixed and working capital costs for COVID-19 adaptation (US$29.5M), plus grant administration and technical assistance (US$10.5M)
Technical assistance for legal/regulatory framework development (US$2M) and capitalization of Deposit Insurance Fund (US$15M) to enhance payout capacity
Modernization of Core Banking System, automation of banking supervision, IT for deposit insurance operationalization, and upgrade of collateral and credit registries

key_components.component_name: Component 5: Strengthening DAB's Regulatory and Supervisory Capacity
key_components.description: Technical assistance for bank resolution framework, banking supervision, financial consumer protection, AML/CFT implementation, and internal audit strengthening
Project management, monitoring, evaluation, and financial audit support for Components 3-6
Contingency component for immediate response to eligible crisis or emergency affecting the financial sector

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Ministry of Finance (MOF) for Components 1-2; Da Afghanistan Bank (DAB) for Components 3-6. Afghan Credit Guarantee Foundation (ACGF) as implementing partner for Components 1-2
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Committee chaired by DAB Governor with heads of relevant technical departments; Implementing Partner Agreement between MOF and ACGF; Subsidiary Agreement between MOF and DAB; coordination with IMF, IFC, KfW Development Bank, UNODC, and bilateral donors
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: FSSPD at DAB handles procurement up to US$100,000 using National Shopping and CQS methods; National Procurement Authority (NPA) handles procurement above US$100,000; provisional arrangement with plan to return full responsibility to DAB upon verification of capacity criteria; STEP system mandatory for all procurement
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Quarterly progress reports from FSSPD and MOF; monitoring against PDO and intermediate indicators; ACGF responsible for data collection on credit guarantee and matching grant activities; survey of MSME borrowers and matching grant recipients at project midterm; Third-Party Monitoring Agent (TPMA) reviews Statement of Expenditures; annual internal audits by MOF and DAB; annual external audits by Supreme Audit Office (SAO)

identified_risks.risk_description: High political and governance risk due to ongoing conflict, political uncertainty, power-sharing disputes, and uncertain international aid pledges expiring in December 2020. Recent US-Taliban agreement has not led to sustained peace. Presidential election disputes and administrative disruption risks
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Implementation through private sector management (ACGF) and partnership with technical institution (DAB); regular implementation support missions; coordination with development partners; project design addresses COVID-19 impacts

identified_risks.risk_description: High macroeconomic risk exacerbated by COVID-19 pandemic with expected GDP contraction of at least 5.5% in 2020, poverty rate spike from 55% to 72%, fiscal pressures with government revenues declining 30%, and weaknesses in financial sector including state-owned bank risks
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project activities specifically designed to address macroeconomic fragility and recession through firm-level interventions (loans and grants) and regulatory-level interventions (institutional capacity strengthening); financial stability reforms to respond to shocks

identified_risks.risk_description: High risk from complexity of reforms requiring multiple stakeholders and institutional involvement. Key risk is enactment of Deposit Insurance Law requiring Parliament involvement. Multiple reform areas with interdependencies
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Early engagement with DAB and ADIC to commence technical work; target law enactment by end 2022; regular monitoring during implementation support missions; proactive actions including project restructuring if needed; close coordination with policy-based lending; ACGF's strong track record under Access to Finance Project; continuous World Bank technical guidance

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial risk in matching grants administration including: double dipping by SMEs, moral hazard (theft, fraud, rapid resale of equipment), inflated proposal prices, non-transparency in selection, security issues, and pandemic-related supply chain disruptions
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Detailed Matching Grants Operations Manual with clear eligibility and selection criteria; public disclosure of beneficiary SMEs; project registry of assets; prohibition of multiple grants to same SME; grant cancellation and asset claims for moral hazard; ineligible cost categories; reimbursement-based payments to bank accounts; market-based cost estimation; third-party monitoring; external audits; continuous monitoring of security and pandemic impacts with program adjustments

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial risk from DAB's limited capacity for procurement and contract management. Previous FSRRP experienced delays and contract management challenges. Difficulty attracting qualified consultants due to security concerns
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Novel dual procurement authority arrangement (FSSPD for <US$100K, NPA for >US$100K); provisional arrangement with plan to return full responsibility upon verification of capacity criteria; review of implementation progress within one year; alternative arrangements if needed; Statement of Expenditure-based disbursement with TPMA review; remote support by qualified consultants using technology; Bank-executed advisory work; regular technical and fiduciary guidance

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial fiduciary risk from low budget utilization (FSRRP closed with only 48% utilization), weak contract management, weak internal controls, and potential fraud/corruption in matching grants administration
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Budget committee establishment within one month of effectiveness; quarterly budget utilization reviews; Statement of Expenditure-based disbursement with TPMA prior review; segregation of duties; Financial Management Manual compliance; internal audits by MOF and DAB; external audits by SAO; ACGF independent external audits; third-party monitoring; anti-corruption declarations from staff and beneficiaries; confidentiality agreements for evaluation committees; World Bank prior review of staffing and operating costs; STEP system for procurement tracking; online complaint management system

identified_risks.risk_description: Ongoing violent conflict and security situation in Afghanistan affects implementation capacity, staff safety, and project activities. COVID-19 pandemic compounds security risks
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project design accounts for security issues; implementation through ACGF and DAB with established presence; remote support options; regular security assessments; contingency planning; flexibility in implementation arrangements

identified_risks.risk_description: Moderate risk from MSME activities that may have environmental and social impacts. Potential risks include environmental pollution, waste management, occupational health and safety issues
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: ACGF Environmental and Social Management System (ESMS) in place and compliant with ESF; E&S provisions in ACGF-PFI contractual agreements; PFI capacity building on E&S risk management; screening tools for MSME proposals; monitoring and reporting on E&S risks; updated guidelines and checklists; staff training; Environmental and Social Commitment Plan (ESCP) prepared

triggered_safeguard_policies: ESS1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
ESS10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - ACGF has established grievance mechanism allowing final beneficiaries to lodge complaints with PFIs; serious or unresolved complaints addressed by ACGF; World Bank Grievance Redress Service available; World Bank Inspection Panel available for complaints about Bank non-compliance
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Women-owned MSMEs are particularly underserved (only 2% of current MSME financing). Project includes specific interventions: technical assistance to PFIs to expand outreach to women; partial credit guarantees for women entrepreneurs; gender-disaggregated reporting; women-owned SMEs encouraged to apply for matching grants with potential extra evaluation points; target of 4% of ACGF-guaranteed loans to women-owned/managed MSMEs by project end (up from 1% baseline); target of 5% of matching grants to women-owned/operated SMEs; DAB to design financial sector reporting requirements on financial services to women
vulnerable_groups: Poor and vulnerable households (55% poverty rate, 93% living on <US$2/day)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project is designed with conflict sensitivity in mind. Implementation through private sector (ACGF) and technical institution (DAB) reduces political risk. Project addresses financial sector stability which is critical for economic recovery in conflict-affected context. Matching grants and credit guarantees provide economic relief to MSMEs affected by conflict and COVID-19. Project includes contingency component for emergency response. Coordination with development partners to ensure complementarity. Project recognizes that conflict affects access to finance through disrupted markets, price volatility, and forced displacement
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Afghanistan is classified as a fragile and conflict-affected state. Key fragility factors: ongoing violent conflict between Taliban and government forces with no clear path to sustained peace; political instability with power-sharing disputes; weak state institutions with limited capacity; high poverty (55% in 2017, expected to spike to 72% due to COVID-19); low human capital index (0.39, ranking 133/157); high vulnerability to shocks (3 in 4 poor households affected by at least one shock); limited financial intermediation (credit to GDP ratio of 3.3%, among lowest in world); weak financial sector with legacy issues from Kabul Bank crisis; dependence on grants (43% of GDP, 75% of public spending); uncertain international aid pledges; COVID-19 pandemic exacerbating all challenges
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Ongoing violent conflict and security situation affects project implementation. Security concerns limit availability of qualified consultants. Project design includes remote support options and flexibility. Implementation through established institutions (DAB, ACGF) with security infrastructure. Contingent Emergency Response Component available for crisis response. Regular security assessments and contingency planning required. COVID-19 pandemic compounds security risks through lockdowns and movement restrictions
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - Afghanistan faces significant displacement and refugee issues. Drought-induced displacement reached record levels of nearly 300,000 individuals. Large influx of hundreds of thousands of people crossing back into Afghanistan from Iran exacerbated COVID-19 situation. Project indirectly addresses displacement impacts through: access to finance for MSMEs to support economic recovery and job creation; financial stability reforms to maintain banking system confidence; matching grants for SME recovery. Project recognizes that conflict-affected populations have limited access to finance and are vulnerable to shocks
